author: @laurelkaye6313
text: Thanks for sharing your thoughts on these things. I always learn something new, or see things a little differently at the end of your videos, and I deeply appreciate your willingness to have these discussions. I don't agree with everything you say, but listening anyway I have found is an excellent way to whet my own beliefs by considering why and how we differ. All the best to you.

author: @matthewnicholson1560
text: You are right in that Lithium is not a significant proportion of the Earth's crust, however there is an awful lot of crust and sufficient of it is lithium to make all the batteries we need. The shortage is mining and refining capacity. Provided we recycle the lithium in batteries that die we will be OK. The big mistake is using lithium for non transport applications which unfortunately we are doing. I have two big lithium batteries. 1 in my car and the other on the wall of my house. In the car - sensible, but fixed to the wall of my house - not so sensible because it is not a  weight or volume sensitive application. Perhaps a sodium battery would make more sense for static storage.

author: @mark.guitar
text: Great stuff mate. I'm a luthier yet I've been looking at tone circuits as ideal capacitors. Maybe the "tone snobs", the most extreme are roundly ridiculed, have a point that certain components have a "special sound". How pronounced this is is now going to be a part of my current guitar build. Small socket for the different types of caps and record the resulting sounds. Obvs the +/- 5% on the component manufacturing is an issue...

author: @AlienLivesMatter
text: Many distributed technologies is better than singular dominant technology except for shareholders of corporations hell bent on domination.

author: @Ken00001010
text: I don't know if you have covered the "photo battery" in one of your earlier videos, but if not, I think you would be interested. In this technology a photo-chemical reaction restores the charge in the battery. There has been quite a bit of work on these in India, and you can find that on the Web. The idea is that you could unroll a mat (or have clothing) in the sun during the day, and then plug electrical appliances into the mat at night. Of course, that would have to be made to be lower cost than the conventional combination of a solar panel plus a rechargeable battery, which is not easy with mass production driving both of those down so quickly. I would think that if there were any practical application it may be in the form of a flow battery style where the photo-chemical solution is pumped through tubing that is out in the sun to activate, and then through a series of half-cells to produce the electricity. But again, hard to beat a solar panel and rechargeable battery for cost.

author: @Renew_You
text: Right on, Rob. I love lithium-ion batteries however looking at them from commodity standpoint, sustainability availability and the fact that they need to be at specific temperatures to charge and discharge they happen not to be suitable for my applications. So, finding the best for me has been a journey. Truth to my way of thinking is full disclosure. We live in a Yankee trader environment that "buyer beware" in that pertinent information is very hazy. Marketeers ignorantly will remove relevant context for fear of any negative classified information released will harm their corporate campaigns. Most of us want the latest and greatest. Sears Roebuck would advertise Good, Better and Best. I've found that good often times is better than better and good actually is better than the best! In our world of consumers most don't care about how or why something works it seems that pushing the button and it does the job as advertised isn't enough either. 🤣🤣🤣

author: @cosmicseekin
text: Your message is well received.  However,  for me it would be very helpful to know if I am viewing 1/9000 of the possible outcome, a crum or the whole biscuite.  or perhaps 1/2 of the biscuit.  That is the difference between the Free Energy geeks and the Flow Battery developers.  Thank you.
